questions.question: A hash function takes binary data and produces a condensed representation. What are the common names for this output?
questions.option_a: A. Secret key, Public key, or Private key
questions.option_b: B. Ciphertext, Plaintext, or Digest
questions.option_c: C. Hash value, Message digest, or Digital fingerprint
questions.option_d: D. Salt, Pepper, or Token
questions.explanation: The output of a hash function is commonly called a hash value, message digest, or digital fingerprint, referring to the fixed-length representation of input data.

questions.question: Hashing is based on a one-way mathematical function. What does 'one-way' mean in this context?
questions.option_a: A. The hash can only be computed once per message
questions.option_b: B. It is relatively easy to compute, but significantly harder to reverse
questions.option_c: C. Only one party can compute the hash at a time
questions.option_d: D. The function can only process data in one direction per session
questions.explanation: A one-way hash function is easy to compute but very difficult to reverse, meaning the original input cannot feasibly be derived from the hash value.

questions.question: Hashing is designed to verify and ensure which two security services?
questions.option_a: A. Confidentiality and Non-repudiation
questions.option_b: B. Availability and Accountability
questions.option_c: C. Data integrity and Authentication
questions.option_d: D. Encryption and Decryption
questions.explanation: Hashing ensures data integrity by detecting modifications and can support authentication when combined with secret keys.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid application of cryptographic hash functions?
questions.option_a: A. Providing proof of authenticity when used with IPsec or routing protocol authentication
questions.option_b: B. Generating one-time and one-way responses to challenges in PPP CHAP
questions.option_c: C. Encrypting sensitive data so only the intended recipient can read it
questions.option_d: D. Providing a message integrity check proof when accessing a secure website
questions.explanation: Hash functions are used for integrity and authentication, not confidentiality. Encryption is required to keep data secret.

questions.question: What is a key limitation of hash functions that makes them vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks?
questions.option_a: A. They produce variable-length digests that are hard to compare
questions.option_b: B. They are too slow for real-time network use
questions.option_c: C. There is no unique identifying information from the sender — anyone with the hash function can compute a hash for any data
questions.option_d: D. They require a public key infrastructure to operate correctly
questions.explanation: Plain hash functions lack sender authentication because anyone can generate a hash for any message.

questions.question: A cryptographic hash function always produces output of what characteristic?
questions.option_a: A. Variable length, depending on the size of the input
questions.option_b: B. Fixed length, regardless of the size of the input
questions.option_c: C. Length equal to the input divided by a constant
questions.option_d: D. Length that doubles with each additional byte of input
questions.explanation: Hash functions always generate a fixed-length digest regardless of the size of the input message.

questions.question: Who developed the MD5 hashing algorithm?
questions.option_a: A. Bruce Schneier
questions.option_b: B. Whitfield Diffie
questions.option_c: C. Ron Rivest
questions.option_d: D. NIST
questions.explanation: MD5 was developed by Ron Rivest and produces a 128-bit message digest.

questions.question: What is the size of the message digest produced by the MD5 algorithm?
questions.option_a: A. 64 bits
questions.option_b: B. 128 bits
questions.option_c: C. 160 bits
questions.option_d: D. 256 bits
questions.explanation: MD5 generates a 128-bit message digest regardless of input size.

questions.question: How many computation steps does the MD5 algorithm involve?
questions.option_a: A. 32 steps
questions.option_b: B. 48 steps
questions.option_c: C. 64 steps
questions.option_d: D. 80 steps
questions.explanation: MD5 uses 64 computation steps during its hashing process.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly compares MD5 and SHA-1?
questions.option_a: A. MD5 is slower and more secure; SHA-1 is faster and less secure
questions.option_b: B. Both MD5 and SHA-1 use a 160-bit buffer
questions.option_c: C. MD5 uses a 128-bit buffer and 64 steps; SHA-1 uses a 160-bit buffer and 80 steps
questions.option_d: D. MD5 is based on SHA-2; SHA-1 is based on MD4
questions.explanation: MD5 uses a 128-bit buffer and 64 steps, while SHA-1 uses a 160-bit buffer and 80 steps.

questions.question: Which organization developed SHA and published the Secure Hash Standard (SHS)?
questions.option_a: A. IEEE
questions.option_b: B. MIT Lincoln Laboratory
questions.option_c: C. U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
questions.option_d: D. IETF
questions.explanation: NIST developed SHA and published it as part of the Secure Hash Standard.

questions.question: SHA-1 was published in 1994. What was the reason for releasing SHA-1?
questions.option_a: A. To replace MD5 entirely with a faster algorithm
questions.option_b: B. To correct an unpublished flaw in the original SHA
questions.option_c: C. To meet government mandates for 256-bit security
questions.option_d: D. To add symmetric key support to hash functions
questions.explanation: SHA-1 was introduced to correct an unpublished flaw found in the original SHA algorithm.

questions.question: What is the maximum message length that SHA-1 can process?
questions.option_a: A. Less than 2^32 bits
questions.option_b: B. Less than 2^48 bits
questions.option_c: C. Less than 2^64 bits
questions.option_d: D. Less than 2^128 bits
questions.explanation: SHA-1 can process messages up to less than 2^64 bits in length.

questions.question: NIST published four additional hash functions in the SHA family beyond SHA-1. Which of the following correctly lists all four?
questions.explanation: The SHA-2 family includes SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, and SHA-512.

questions.question: Why is SHA-1 considered more secure than MD5 even though it is slower?
questions.option_a: A. SHA-1 uses a private key while MD5 does not
questions.option_b: B. SHA-1 can process encrypted input directly
questions.option_c: C. SHA-1's larger 160-bit message digest makes it more resistant to brute-force attacks
questions.option_d: D. SHA-1 was developed by a government agency
questions.explanation: SHA-1 generates a larger 160-bit digest compared to MD5's 128-bit digest, making brute-force attacks more difficult.

questions.question: What does HMAC stand for, and what makes it different from a plain hash function?
questions.option_a: A. Hashed Message Authentication Code; it uses asymmetric keys
questions.option_b: B. Hybrid Message Authentication Code; it combines two hash algorithms
questions.option_c: C. Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code; it uses a secret key as input
questions.option_d: D. Hash-based MAC; it replaces the hash function entirely
questions.explanation: HMAC uses a secret key together with a hash function to provide both integrity and authentication.

questions.question: Why does HMAC guarantee authenticity in a way that a plain hash function cannot?
questions.option_a: A. HMAC uses a longer digest length
questions.option_b: B. HMAC is based on asymmetric cryptography
questions.option_c: C. Only the sender and receiver know the secret key used to compute the HMAC
questions.option_d: D. HMAC timestamps every message
questions.explanation: Because the secret key is shared only by sender and receiver, attackers cannot generate a valid HMAC.

questions.question: On what three factors does the cryptographic strength of an HMAC depend?
questions.option_a: A. Hash algorithm version, message length, and network protocol
questions.option_b: B. Strength of the hash function, key size and quality, and output size
questions.option_c: C. Number of iterations, block size, and initialization vector
questions.option_d: D. Key expiry time, certificate chain, and cipher suite
questions.explanation: HMAC security depends on the strength of the underlying hash function, the key size and quality, and the size of the hash output.

questions.question: Cisco technologies use two well-known HMAC functions. Which correctly names them?
questions.option_a: A. HMAC-AES and HMAC-3DES
questions.option_b: B. HMAC-RSA and HMAC-DSA
questions.option_c: C. HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1
questions.option_d: D. HMAC-SHA-256 and HMAC-SHA-512
questions.explanation: Cisco technologies commonly use HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1 for authentication and integrity verification.

questions.question: In HMAC verification, what does it mean if the generated HMAC matches the received HMAC?
questions.option_a: A. The message was encrypted using AES
questions.option_b: B. The message was compressed before transmission
questions.option_c: C. Both integrity and authenticity of the message are verified
questions.option_d: D. The message was digitally signed with a public key
questions.explanation: Matching HMAC values confirm the message was not altered and was generated by someone with the shared secret key.

questions.question: How does HMAC defeat man-in-the-middle attacks compared to a plain hash function?
questions.option_a: A. HMAC encrypts the message payload
questions.option_b: B. HMAC uses public-key infrastructure
questions.option_c: C. HMAC requires a shared secret key that attackers do not know
questions.option_d: D. HMAC embeds the sender's IP address in the digest
questions.explanation: An attacker cannot recompute a valid HMAC without knowing the shared secret key.

questions.question: Key management is considered the most difficult part of designing a cryptosystem. Which list correctly shows the five key management characteristics?
questions.option_a: A. Key creation, backup, deployment, update, logging
questions.option_b: B. Key generation, verification, storage, exchange, revocation and destruction
questions.option_c: C. Key issuing, hashing, signing, rotation, archiving
questions.option_d: D. Key provisioning, escrow, wrapping, splitting, shredding
questions.explanation: Key management includes generation, verification, storage, exchange, and revocation or destruction of keys.

questions.question: What is the purpose of key verification in cryptographic systems?
questions.option_a: A. To confirm both parties received the same key
questions.option_b: B. To encrypt the key before storing it
questions.option_c: C. To detect and regenerate weak keys that should not be used
questions.option_d: D. To digitally sign the key with a certificate authority
questions.explanation: Key verification identifies weak or insecure keys and ensures stronger keys are used.

questions.question: What is the difference between key revocation and key destruction?
questions.option_a: A. Revocation backs up the key while destruction archives it
questions.option_b: B. Revocation notifies parties a key is compromised; destruction permanently erases it
questions.option_c: C. Revocation generates a new key; destruction deletes expired keys
questions.option_d: D. They are the same process with different names
questions.explanation: Revocation informs systems a key should no longer be used, while destruction permanently removes it.

questions.question: What is the relationship between key length and keyspace?
questions.option_a: A. Keyspace increases linearly with key length
questions.option_b: B. Keyspace decreases as key length increases
questions.option_c: C. Keyspace increases exponentially; each additional bit doubles the keyspace
questions.option_d: D. Keyspace and key length are unrelated
questions.explanation: Keyspace equals 2 raised to the key length, meaning each additional bit doubles possible keys.

questions.question: If a 56-bit key has about 72 quadrillion possible keys, how many does a 57-bit key have?
questions.option_a: A. 72 quadrillion
questions.option_b: B. 108 quadrillion
questions.option_c: C. 144 quadrillion
questions.option_d: D. 216 quadrillion
questions.explanation: Adding one bit doubles the keyspace, so 57 bits gives approximately 144 quadrillion possibilities.

questions.question: What tradeoff must administrators consider when choosing a cryptographic key length?
questions.option_a: A. Longer keys are easier to remember
questions.option_b: B. Shorter keys require more storage
questions.option_c: C. Longer keys are more secure but require more processing resources
questions.option_d: D. Key length only affects asymmetric algorithms
questions.explanation: Longer keys increase security but also increase computational cost and may reduce system performance.

questions.question: What are the four types of cryptographic keys described in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. Session, Master, Ephemeral, Pre-shared keys
questions.option_b: B. Symmetric keys, Asymmetric keys, Digital signatures, Hash keys
questions.option_c: C. Public keys, Private keys, Group keys, Broadcast keys
questions.option_d: D. Encryption keys, Decryption keys, Signing keys, Verification keys
questions.explanation: The lecture identifies symmetric keys, asymmetric keys, digital signatures, and hash keys as key categories.

questions.question: According to the cryptographic algorithm table, which algorithms are used for data integrity?
questions.option_a: A. DES and AES
questions.option_b: B. HMAC-MD5 and HMAC-SHA-1
questions.option_c: C. RSA and DSA
questions.option_d: D. MD5 and SHA
questions.explanation: MD5 and SHA are hashing algorithms used to verify data integrity.

questions.question: A security policy requires strong authentication. Which combination of algorithms is most appropriate?
questions.option_a: A. DES and 3DES
questions.option_b: B. MD5 and SHA
questions.option_c: C. HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, RSA, and DSA
questions.option_d: D. AES-128 and AES-256
questions.explanation: HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, RSA, and DSA are commonly used algorithms for authentication.

questions.question: Which key sizes are recommended for protection up to 10 years across different key types?
questions.option_a: A. Symmetric 80-bit, Asymmetric 1248-bit, Digital Signature 160-bit, Hash 160-bit
questions.option_b: B. Symmetric 96-bit, Asymmetric 1776-bit, Digital Signature 192-bit, Hash 192-bit
questions.option_c: C. Symmetric 128-bit, Asymmetric 3248-bit, Digital Signature 256-bit, Hash 256-bit
questions.option_d: D. Symmetric 256-bit, Asymmetric 15424-bit, Digital Signature 512-bit, Hash 512-bit
questions.explanation: For about 10 years of protection, recommended sizes are Symmetric 96-bit, Asymmetric 1776-bit, Digital Signature 192-bit, and Hash 192-bit.
